articles.title: This Croc Cousin Started on All Fours, Then Walked Like a T-Rex!
articles.summary: Imagine a reptile the size of a poodle that starts life crawling on four legs, then switches to walking on two as an adult! Scientists found fossils of a weird crocodile relative called Sonselasuchus cedrus in Arizona. By studying its leg bones, they discovered it grew longer hind legs as it got older, letting it stand upright. This creature lived over 200 million years ago and had a toothless beak and hollow bones, just like some dinosaurs—but it wasn't a dinosaur at all. It's a super cool example of how different animals can evolve the same features.

articles.title: Giant Underground Cave Found on Venus—Bigger Than Any on Earth!
articles.summary: Scientists found a huge lava tube on Venus by looking at old radar images from a 1990s NASA probe. The cave is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide—that's bigger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars! It formed when molten lava flowed underground and then drained away, leaving an empty tunnel. A collapsed "skylight" hole near a volcano called Nyx Mons gave it away. This discovery is super exciting because it shows there might be more caves on Venus, and it will help future missions like NASA's VERITAS and Europe's EnVision explore the planet in even more detail.

articles.title: 289-Million-Year-Old Mummy Reptile Reveals How We Started Breathing
articles.summary: A tiny, mummified reptile found in an Oklahoma cave is teaching scientists how land animals first learned to breathe with their ribs. The fossil, called Captorhinus aguti, is 289 million years old and so well-preserved that it still has skin, cartilage, and even traces of ancient proteins! High-tech scans showed that this lizard-like creature had a rib-based breathing system—the same kind we use today. Before this, early land animals breathed through their skin and used their mouths to push air into their lungs. Rib breathing was a game-changer because it let animals be more active. This discovery pushes back the oldest known proteins by nearly 100 million years!
